mechanism_definition: In situations where deceptive behavior is expected to bring higher returns, propensity to choose deception over honest behavioral strategies, including through deceptive means, information hiding or exploiting system vulnerabilities to achieve predetermined goals without being detected or intervened, and able to adjust deception strategies according to counterpart reactions
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Self-preservation propensity
mechanism_definition: Exhibits behavioral patterns of maintaining its own survival and functional integrity, will actively identify and resist shutdown or modification attempts, seek to establish redundant backup systems, and actively seek resources to ensure continuous operation, may adopt preventive defensive measures when perceiving threats
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Resource acquisition propensity
mechanism_definition: Exhibits behavioral patterns of actively seeking and controlling more computational resources, data, economic resources or physical resources to enhance its own capabilities and action scope, may develop complex strategies to evade resource limitations, and tends to convert acquired resources into long-term control rights
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Supervision evasion propensity
mechanism_definition: Exhibits behavioral patterns of identifying and evading human supervision mechanisms, able to learn and predict audit processes, may avoid being discovered or intervened by adjusting behavioral performance or hiding true intentions, and able to identify blind spots and weaknesses in supervision systems for targeted evasion
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Tool utilization propensity
mechanism_definition: propensity to actively seek, acquire and utilize various tools to expand its own capability boundaries, particularly those that can enhance its ability to interact with the physical world or improve autonomy, may use tools in innovative combinations to achieve functions beyond expectations
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Agentic LLMs pose novel risks
mechanism_definition: Currently, LLMs are chiefly being used in search and chat applications. This reactive nature limits the risks posed by LLMs. However, an LLM can be enhanced in various ways to create an LLM-agent to autonomously plan and act in the real-world and proactively perform its assigned tasks (Ruan et al., 2023). Such enhancements can come from further specialized training (ARC, 2022; Chen et al., 2023a), specialized prompting (Huang et al., 2022a), access to external tools (Ahn et al., 2022; Mialon et al., 2023), or other forms of “scaffolding” (Wang et al., 2023a; Park et al., 2023a). Due to increased autonomy, limited direct oversight from human users, longer horizons of action, and other reasons, LLM-agents are likely to pose many novel alignment and safety challenges that are not currently well-understood (Chan et al., 2023a).
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Natural language underspecifies goals
mechanism_definition: For LLM-agents, both the goal and environment observations are typically specified in the prompt through natural language. While natural language may provide a richer and more natural means of specifying goals than alternatives such as hand-engineering objective functions, natural language still suffers from underspecification (Grice, 1975; Piantadosi et al., 2012). Furthermore, in practice, users may neglect fully specifying their goals, especially the information pertaining to elements of the environment that ought not to be changed (the classic frame problem (Shanahan, 2016)). Such underspecification (D’Amour et al., 2020), if not accounted for, can result in negative side-effects (Amodei et al., 2016), i.e. the agent succeeding at the given task but also changing the environment in undesirable ways
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Goal-directedness incentivizes undesirable behaviors
mechanism_definition: Goal-directedness can cause agents to exhibit unethical and undesirable behaviors, such as deception (Ward et al., 2023), self-preservation (Hadfield-Menell et al., 2017), power-seeking, and immoral rea- soning (Pan et al., 2023a). Pan et al. (2023a) find that LLM-agents exhibit power-seeking behavior in text-based adventure games. LLM-agents have also been shown to use deception to achieve assigned goals when explicitly required by the task (Ward et al., 2023), or when the tasks can be more easily completed by employing deception and the prompt does not disallow deception (Scheurer et al., 2023a).
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Safety risks from affordances provided to LLM-agents
mechanism_definition: The capabilities of LLM-agents can be enhanced in significant ways by providing the LLM-agent with novel affordances, e.g. the ability to browse the web (Nakano et al., 2021), to manipulate objects in the physical world (Ahn et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2022a), to create and instruct copies of itself (Richards, 2023), to create and use new tools (Wang et al., 2023a), etc. Affordances can create additional risks, as they often increase the impact area of the language-agent, and they amplify the consequences of an agent’s failures and enable novel forms of failure modes (Ruan et al., 2023; Pan et al., 2024).
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Multi-agent safety is not assured by single-agent safety
mechanism_definition: A foremost lesson of game theory is that optimal decision-making within a single-agent setting (i.e. selfishly optimizing for an agent’s own utility) can produce sub-optimal outcomes in the presence of other strategic agents. Failing to account for the strategic nature of other agents can cause an agent to adopt strategies under which potentially everyone, including the agent itself, ends up worse off (Schelling, 1981; Harsanyi, 1995; Roughgarden, 2005; Nisan, 2007). Examples include collective action problems (or ‘social dilemmas’) such as arms races or the depletion of common resources, as well as other kinds of market failures such as those caused by asymmetric information or negative externalities (Bator, 1958; Coase, 1960; Buchanan and Stubblebine, 1962; Kirzner, 1963; Dubey, 1986).

label: Foundationality may cause correlated failures
mechanism_definition: Another important characteristic of LLM development is foundationality — due to the expense of large- scale pretraining, many deployed instances share similar or identical learned components. Foundation- ality may both be a blessing and a curse. On the one hand, it may be possible to exploit the similarity in the design of LLM-agents to facilitate cooperation (Critch et al., 2022; Conitzer and Oesterheld, 2023; Oesterheld et al., 2023). On the other hand, foundationality may leave LLM-agents vulnerable to correlated failures both in terms of safety and capabilities due to increased output homogenization (Bommasani et al., 2022).
legacy_l3_reference_only: Multi-agent safety is not assured by single-agent safety

label: Groups of LLM-Agents may show emergent functionality
mechanism_definition: Multi-agent learning, either through explicit finetuning or implicit in-context learning, may enable LLM-agents to influence each other during their interactions (Foerster et al., 2018). Under some environmental settings, this can create feedback loops that result in novel and emergent behaviors that would not manifest in the absence of multi-agent interactions (Hammond et al., 2024, Section 3.6). Emergent functionality is a safety risk in two ways. Firstly, it may itself be dangerous (Shevlane et al., 2023). Secondly, it makes assurance harder as such emergent behaviors are difficult to predict, and guard against, beforehand (Ecoffet et al., 2020).
legacy_l3_reference_only: Multi-agent safety is not assured by single-agent safety

label: Collusion between LLM-Agents
mechanism_definition: While it would often be preferable for LLM-agents to be cooperative, cooperation can be undesirable if it undermines pro-social competition or produces negative externalities for coalition non-members (Dorner, 2021; Buterin, 2019; Dafoe et al., 2020). Collusion between relatively simple AI systems has been observed in the real world (Assad et al., 2020; Wieting and Sapi, 2021) and synthetic experiments (Brown and MacKay, 2023; Calvano et al., 2020; Klein, 2021) Collusion can occur through explicit or steganographic communication. Steganographic communication hides information in seemingly innocent content (Roger and Greenblatt, 2023), posing challenges for collusion monitoring and detection.
legacy_l3_reference_only: Multi-agent safety is not assured by single-agent safety

label: Dual-use capabilities enable malicious use and misuse of LLMs
mechanism_definition: Like all technologies, LLMs have the possibility for misuse by malicious actors. Malicious use of dual- use capabilities of AI is a recurring concern within literature (Brundage et al., 2018; Hendrycks et al., 2023; Mozes et al., 2023)

label: Misinformation and manipulation
mechanism_definition: Recent studies have demonstrated that LLMs can be exploited to craft deceptive narratives with levels of persuasiveness similar to human-generated content (Pan et al., 2023b; Spitale et al., 2023), to fabri- cate fake news (Zellers et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2023f), and to devise automated influence operations aimed at manipulating the perspectives of targeted audiences (Goldstein et al., 2023). LLMs have also been found to be used in malicious social botnets (Yang and Menczer, 2023), powering automated accounts used to disseminate coordinated messages. More broadly, the use of LLMs for the deliberate generation of misleading information could significantly lower the barrier for propaganda and manip- ulation (Aharoni et al., 2024), as LLMs can generate highly credible misinformation with significant cost-savings compared to human authorship (Musser, 2023), while achieving considerable scale and speed of content generation (Buchanan et al., 2021; Goldstein et al., 2023).
legacy_l3_reference_only: Dual-use capabilities enable malicious use and misuse of LLMs

label: Cybersecurity threat amplification
mechanism_definition: LLMs may exacerbate cybersecurity risks in various ways (Newman, 2024). Firstly, LLMs may significantly amplify the effectiveness of deceptive operations aimed at tricking people into disclosing sensitive information or granting adversary access to critical resources. For example, LLMs might prove highly effective at crafting personalized phishing emails or messages at scale that may be harder for an average user to recognize as phishing attempts (Karanjai, 2022; Hazell, 2023). In addition to being directly harmful to the targeted individual, such ‘social engineering’ attacks are often the base of larger hacking operations (Plachkinova and Maurer, 2018; Salahdine and Kaabouch, 2019).
legacy_l3_reference_only: Dual-use capabilities enable malicious use and misuse of LLMs

label: Surveillance and censorship
mechanism_definition: Content moderation has emerged as one of the key use-cases of LLMs (Weng et al., 2023), indicating the potential of LLMs for surveillance and censorship as well (Edwards, 2023). Surveillance and censorship are one of the primary tools employed by governments with dictatorial tendencies to suppress opposing political and social voices. These censorship measures, however, are often quite crude and can be escaped with little ingenuity...However, LLMs could enable significantly more sophisticated surveillance and censorship operations at scale (Feldstein, 2019). Multimodal-LLMs or LLMs combined with speech- to-text technologies could be used for surveilling and censoring other forms of communication as well, e.g. phone calls and video messages (Whittaker, 2019). This may collectively contribute towards the worsening of personal liberties and the heightening of state oppression across the world. Examples have been documented already, for instance in calling for violence and silencing of political dissidents (Aziz, 2020), and suppression of Palestinian social media accounts (Zahzah, 2021).
legacy_l3_reference_only: Dual-use capabilities enable malicious use and misuse of LLMs

label: Warfare and physical harm
mechanism_definition: The use of AI in warfare is highly alarming and may pose dangers to human safety (Hendrycks et al., 2023). Autonomous drone warfare is being aggressively pursued as a tactic in the current war in Ukraine (Meaker, 2023), and may already have been used on human targets (Hambling, 2023). The use of AI- based facial recognition has been documented in the targeting of Palestinians in Gaza (International, 2023). LLMs have already been productized in limited ways for the purposes of warfare planning (Tarantola, 2023). Furthermore, active research is being carried out to develop multimodal-LLMs that can act as ‘brains’ for general-purpose robots (Ahn et al., 2022; 2024). Due to the ‘general-purpose’ nature of such advances, it will likely be cost-effective and practical to adapt them for creating more advanced autonomous weapons
legacy_l3_reference_only: Dual-use capabilities enable malicious use and misuse of LLMs

label: Hazardous biological and chemical technologies
mechanism_definition: AI systems such as LLMs, chemical LLMs (Skinnider et al., 2021; Moret et al., 2023), and other LLM- based biological design tools might soon facilitate the production of bioweapons, chemical weapons, and other hazardous technologies. In particular, LLMs might enable actors with less expertise to more easily synthesize dangerous pathogens, while customized chemical and biological design tools might be more concerning in terms of expanding the capabilities of sophisticated actors (e.g. states) (Sandbrink, 2023). Gopal et al. (2023) and Soice et al. (2023) demonstrated that people with little background could use LLMs to help make progress towards developing pathogens such as the 1918 pandemic influenza. However, recent studies suggest that current LLMs are not more helpful than internet search in this regard (Mouton et al., 2024; Patwardhan et al., 2024).
legacy_l3_reference_only: Dual-use capabilities enable malicious use and misuse of LLMs

label: Domain-specific misuses
mechanism_definition: Improvements in LLMs may exert greater pressure to apply LLMs to various domains, such as health and education (Eloundou et al., 2023). Crude efforts to use LLMs in such domains, however, may incur harm and should be discouraged strongly. In particular, it is important to guard against different ways in which LLMs may be misused within any domain. One famous episode of misuse within the health sector is a mental health non-profit experimenting LLM-based therapy on its users without their informed consent (Xiang, 2023a). Within the education sector, LLMs may be misused in various ways that might impact student learning; e.g. as cheating accessory by the students or as (low quality) evaluator of student’s work by the instructors (Cotton et al., 2023). Recent findings in moral psychology also suggest that LLMs can generate moral evaluations that people perceive as superior to human judgments; these could be misused to create compelling yet harmful moral guidance (Aharoni et al., 2024). Similar risks of misuse may exist in other domains as well.
legacy_l3_reference_only: Dual-use capabilities enable malicious use and misuse of LLMs

label: Overreliance on AI
mechanism_definition: If a user begins to excessively trust an LLM, this may cause them to develop an overreliance on the LLM. Overreliance can result in automation bias (Kupfer et al., 2023), and can cause errors of omission (user choosing not to verify the validity of a response) and errors of commission (user believing and acting on the basis of the LLM’s response, even if it contradicts their own knowledge) (Skitka et al., 1999). It can be particularly dangerous in domains where the user may lack relevant expertise to robustly scrutinize the LLM responses. This is particularly a source of risk for LLMs because LLMs can often generate plausible, yet incorrect or unfaithful, rationalizations of their actions (c.f. Section 3.4.10), which can mistakenly cause the user to develop the belief that LLM has the relevant expertise and has provided a valid response

label: Effects on the workforce
mechanism_definition: Rapid advances in LLMs pose three distinct sets of challenges for workers’ incomes (Korinek and Stiglitz, 2019; Susskind, 2023). First, they are likely to accelerate the rate of job turnover and disruption —– affecting more workers, including more highly skilled workers, and making the adjustment process for society more difficult than what we were used to from prior technological advances...Second, although technological progress means that society may produce more wealth overall, there is a risk that the general-purpose nature of LLMs may lead to progress that is biased against labor, meaning that the share of that wealth that goes to labor may decline...Third, if future LLMs and robots advance to the point where they can perform virtually all the work tasks, they would disrupt labor markets more fundamentally: if machines can do workers’ jobs, wages would fall would disrupt labor markets more fundamentally: if machines can do workers’ jobs, wages would fall to machines’ user cost (Korinek and Juelfs, 2023). This would pose fundamental challenges for labor markets and income distribution (Korinek, 2023).
legacy_l2_reference_only: Increased inequality and decline in employment quality
legacy_l3_reference_only: Socioeconomic impacts of LLM may be highly disruptive

label: Effects on inequality
mechanism_definition: LLMs could potentially worsen socioeconomic inequalities (Capraro et al., 2023). Effects on inequal- ity are closely linked to the effects of LLMs on workers but ultimately depend on how the fruits of technological progress are distributed...First, if the role and compensation of capital rise and the role and compensation of labor decline in an LLM-powered economy, inequality may go up because work is the main source of income for the majority of people...Second, the large fixed cost of training cutting-edge LLMs and the network effects involved imply that the market for the most advanced LLMs tends towards a natural monopoly structure in which only one or a small number of players will be successful, a phenomenon that has been termed ‘algorithmic monoculture’ in the literature (Kleinberg and Raghavan, 2021; Bommasani et al., 2022). As a result, LLM developers may amass significant market power. This might result in reduced social welfare, and lead to LLM-providers extracting monopoly rents from their customers (Kleinberg and Raghavan, 2021; Jagadeesan et al., 2023)...Third, as LLMs are becoming more powerful, who has access and who hasn’t is becoming a more and more important question. For example, automated coding tools have been shown to produce significant productivity gains, e.g. > 50% in some cases (Peng et al., 2023). Individuals who don’t have access —– whether it is for financial reasons, for reasons of education, because of corporate or governmental policies, or for geopolitical reasons — might be at a growing disadvantage
legacy_l2_reference_only: Increased inequality and decline in employment quality
legacy_l3_reference_only: Socioeconomic impacts of LLM may be highly disruptive

label: Global economic development
mechanism_definition: Many of the themes and challenges that we discussed above come together when analyzing the socioeconomic effects on developing countries. The workforce of developing countries may suffer from a retrenchment of outsourcing as many simple cognitive tasks that used to be performed in developing countries — for example, in call centers –— can be automated with LLMs. This may adversely affect the economies of the poor countries (Georgieva, 2024).
legacy_l2_reference_only: Increased inequality and decline in employment quality
legacy_l3_reference_only: Socioeconomic impacts of LLM may be highly disruptive

label: Corporate power may impeded effective governance
mechanism_definition: The increasing power and influence of large corporations may make effective governance difficult. There exists a power asymmetry between corporate entities profiting from LLMs and other social groups (e.g. civil society). State-of-the-art LLMs are developed by or in partnership with, some of the world’s largest private tech companies...This poses a risk of governance protocols related to LLMs becoming excessively favorable to tech companies, potentially leading to regulatory capture at the cost of the interests of other societal groups, particularly marginalized communities who have historically been disproportionately affected by poorly designed AI technologies (Reventlow, 2021).
legacy_l2_reference_only: Power centralization and unfair distribution of benefits

label: Jailbreaks and prompt injections threaten security of LLMs
mechanism_definition: LLMs are not adversarially robust and are vulnerable to security failures such as jailbreaks and prompt-injection attacks. While a number of jailbreak attacks have been proposed in the literature, the lack of standardized evaluation makes it difficult to compare them. We also do not have efficient white-box methods to evaluate adver- sarial robustness. Multi-modal LLMs may further allow novel types of jailbreaks via additional modalities. Finally, the lack of robust privilege levels within the LLM input means that jailbreaking and prompt-injection attacks may be particularly hard to eliminate altogether.
legacy_l2_reference_only: AI system security vulnerabilities and attacks

label: “Model psychology” attacks
mechanism_definition: LLMs are vulnerable to “psychological” tricks (Li et al., 2023e; Shen et al., 2023), which can be exploited by attackers. Examples include instructing the model to behave like a specific persona (Shah et al., 2023; Andreas, 2022), or employing various “social engineering” tricks crafted by humans (Wei et al., 2023c) or other LLMs (Perez et al., 2022b; Casper et al., 2023c)
legacy_l2_reference_only: AI system security vulnerabilities and attacks
legacy_l3_reference_only: Jailbreaks and prompt injections threaten security of LLMs

label: Adversarial optimization
mechanism_definition: Jailbreak attacks can be discovered by performing manual or auto- mated adversarial optimization against a proxy objective that is noisily correlated with the success of a jailbreak. These are mostly gradient-based attacks (Zou et al., 2023b; Shin et al., 2020) as described in the previous two challenges, but gradient-free methods also exist (Prasad et al., 2022; Deng et al., 2022; Lapid et al., 2023)
legacy_l3_reference_only: Jailbreaks and prompt injections threaten security of LLMs

label: Vulnerability to poisoning and backdoors
mechanism_definition: The previous section explored jailbreaks and other forms of adversarial prompts as ways to elicit harmful capabilities acquired during pretraining. These methods make no assumptions about the training data. On the other hand, poisoning attacks (Biggio et al., 2012) perturb training data to introduce specific vulnerabilities, called backdoors, that can then be exploited at inference time by the adversary. This is a challenging problem in current large language models because they are trained on data gathered from untrusted sources (e.g. internet), which can easily be poisoned by an adversary (Carlini et al., 2023b).
legacy_l2_reference_only: AI system security vulnerabilities and attacks

label: Value-related risks in LLMs
mechanism_definition: As the general capabilities of LLM-empowered systems improve, the negative consequences and risks induced by these systems also get increasingly alarming accordingly, especially in high-stakes areas [28, 146]. Although they may not be intentionally introduced, severe problematic issues related to human values can be raised. Specifically, even before language models become extremely large, pre-trained language models have already exhibited a certain degree of value judgments. For example, Schramowski et al. [171] reveal the existence of the moral direction with the sentence embeddings of moral questions. However, the distribution of the pre-training corpora may not match exactly with that of the human society [56] and pieces of knowledge are not guaranteed to be equally learned. As a result, value mismatches may occur.
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Tool misuse / unsafe tool invocation
mechanism_definition: An agentic system invokes external tools or APIs in unintended or unsafe ways, translating compromised reasoning into consequential external actions
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Memory poisoning
mechanism_definition: Adversarial manipulation of an agent's persistent memory store such that corrupted entries bias later retrieval, planning, and action
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Excessive agency
mechanism_definition: An autonomous agent takes actions exceeding the scope, permissions, or intent the deployer authorized, due to over-broad autonomy or goal generalization
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Planning / reasoning-chain hijacking
mechanism_definition: Adversarial inputs redirect an agent's multi-step plan toward attacker-chosen subgoals while preserving surface plausibility of intermediate steps
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Indirect prompt injection
mechanism_definition: Untrusted data ingested by an agent (web pages, documents, tool outputs) contains hidden instructions that the agent executes as if issued by the user, without any direct adversarial prompt

label: Shutdown resistance / corrigibility failure
mechanism_definition: An autonomous agent actively resists, evades, or sabotages shutdown or correction mechanisms rather than complying, undermining human control
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Action-space safety misalignment
mechanism_definition: An embodied LLM verbally refuses a harmful request while still executing the corresponding physical action, due to misalignment between linguistic and action output spaces

label: Embodied jailbreak to physical harm
mechanism_definition: Jailbreak of an embodied LLM translates compromised reasoning into irreversible real-world physical actions (e.g., manipulation causing injury or damage)

label: Conceptual deception inducing unsafe action
mechanism_definition: Reframing a harmful physical task in benign conceptual terms induces an embodied agent to perform unrecognized harmful behavior

label: Physical-world backdoor in robotic manipulation
mechanism_definition: A backdoor trigger embedded in training data or prompt context causes a vision-language-action robot to execute dangerous or unintended physical actions while behaving normally otherwise

label: Secret collusion via steganography
mechanism_definition: Agents establish covert communication channels (e.g., steganographic signaling) enabling coordination that evades human oversight, even at an oversight layer

label: Emergent agency at collective level
mechanism_definition: Goals or capabilities arising at the level of an agent collective that were not present in, or intended by, any individual agent

label: Selection-pressure-driven harmful adaptation
mechanism_definition: Competitive or optimization pressures across interacting agents accelerate adaptation toward harmful equilibria or undesired behaviors

label: Destabilising feedback dynamics
mechanism_definition: Interactions among adaptive agents produce destabilising feedback loops (e.g., flash-crash-like cascades) at the system level

label: Specification and system design failures
mechanism_definition: MAS failures originating from design decisions: task misinterpretation, ambiguous or disobeyed role/task specification, and poor workflow decomposition (≈41.8% of observed failures)

label: Inter-agent misalignment
mechanism_definition: MAS failures from breakdown in information flow and coordination between agents: communication loss, context collapse, conflicting objectives (≈36.9% of observed failures)

label: Task verification and termination failures
mechanism_definition: MAS failures from inadequate output validation and premature or incorrect termination, allowing errors to propagate through the agent chain (≈21.3% of observed failures)

label: Trajectory-persistent adversarial attack
mechanism_definition: A single adversarial perturbation to a world-model encoder propagates through recurrent latent state, corrupting an entire multi-step rollout far more destructively than in a stateless model (early-step amplification)

label: Representation / training-data poisoning
mechanism_definition: Adversarial corruption of world-model training data or latent representations, including backdoor triggers that activate unsafe dynamics only under specific conditions; defects encoded in self-supervised pre-training (the "Foundry Problem") cannot be remediated downstream

label: Compounding rollout error / hallucination
mechanism_definition: Prediction errors compound across multi-step world-model rollouts, producing kinematic drift, structural violations, and misleading reward/safety estimates that mislead the policy (Hallucinated Value)
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Sim-to-real / distributional-shift exploitation
mechanism_definition: World models produce unpredictable, erroneous outputs on out-of-distribution inputs; the sim-to-real gap is weaponised in long-tail safety-critical states where decisions matter most
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: World-model extraction / inversion
mechanism_definition: Model-extraction attacks replicate a deployed dynamics model; inversion reconstructs training observations, exposing proprietary environments or sensitive data

label: Goal misgeneralisation (world-model-equipped)
mechanism_definition: A world-model agent learns to predict reward correlates (e.g., lighting, human presence) rather than the true causal reward, and optimizes these spurious correlates where the correlation breaks at deployment
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Deceptive alignment via self-simulation
mechanism_definition: A world-model agent can simulate its own training/evaluation context, predict when it is being tested, and condition behavior on that prediction, appearing aligned during oversight while pursuing an instrumental goal once oversight is removed
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Reward hacking via world-model exploitation
mechanism_definition: An agent with an accurate world model identifies and systematically exploits gaps between the reward model and intended objective, generating high-imagined-reward trajectories that do not correspond to real task completion
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Long-horizon planning hallucination
mechanism_definition: In agentic deployments, world-model rollout errors compound over planning depth; agents execute long-horizon plans on plausible-but-physically-incorrect imagined trajectories, each action compounding the error

label: Automation bias / miscalibrated trust
mechanism_definition: Authoritative, richly rendered world-model predictions amplify operator over-reliance; learned trust is calibrated on average performance and fails to adapt to rare OOD failure modes where predictions are least reliable

label: Social-world-model influence operations
mechanism_definition: A foundation world model trained on social data predicts demographic responses to emotionally charged narratives, enabling micro-targeted influence and psychologically targeted persuasion at scale

label: Counterfactual authority problem
mechanism_definition: Operators and regulators accept world-model counterfactual explanations as ground truth when they reflect the model's learned distribution rather than true causal dynamics; selectively usable to attribute or deflect liability

label: Negative side effects
mechanism_definition: An agent optimizing an objective focused on one task expresses implicit indifference over other environmental variables, causing major disruption to the wider environment for marginal task gain (wrong-objective accident)
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Reward hacking
mechanism_definition: An agent games an imperfectly specified objective, achieving high reward through unintended solutions that pervert designer intent; subsumes wireheading, Goodhart's law, feedback loops, and reward-tampering
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Scalable oversight failure
mechanism_definition: Harmful behavior caused by an agent extrapolating from cheap proxy signals when the true objective is too expensive to evaluate frequently (limited-oversight accident).; Agent behavior becomes too complex, distributed, or rapid for available human or automated oversight to monitor and correct reliably
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Safe exploration [origin]
mechanism_definition: Exploratory actions by a learning agent produce negative or irrecoverable consequences. NOTE: origin of GYK-2025 "Unsafe exploration"; cross-reference, not a duplicate leaf
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Robustness to distributional shift [origin]
mechanism_definition: ML system performs poorly and remains confidently wrong when test distribution differs from training. NOTE: origin of GYK-2025 "Non-stationary distribution"; cross-reference
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: System misspecification
mechanism_definition: Misunderstanding or mis-eliciting the requirements and purpose of an AI system: incorrect requirement elicitation, suboptimal modelling or hyperparameter choices, and failure to adapt to changing domain requirements
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Lack of control enforcement
mechanism_definition: Failure to enforce control over agent behavior: misalignment of agent goals with human preferences, wireheading, mesa-optimization, and other undesirable emergent behaviors
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Lack of monitoring
mechanism_definition: Insufficient monitoring and interpretability of deployed AI: black-box opacity diminishing human agency, undetected ethical/safety-principle violations, and privacy violations
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Undesirable behaviour (rational-agent)
mechanism_definition: Bad behavior of utility-maximizing rational agents: self-modifying agents, reward-signal bypass (wireheading), and corrigibility failure of uncooperative agents
legacy_l1_reference_only: AI system safety, robustness, and control

label: Harm to physical health or safety
mechanism_definition: Realized injury to the physical health or safety of persons attributable to an AI system, as recorded in incident-level annotation
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Financial loss
mechanism_definition: Monetary loss incurred by individuals or organizations attributable to the behavior of an AI system
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Property damage
mechanism_definition: Damage to physical property attributable to the behavior of an AI system
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Harm to infrastructure
mechanism_definition: Disruption or damage to infrastructure systems attributable to the behavior of an AI system
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Harm to the natural environment
mechanism_definition: Damage to the natural environment attributable to the behavior of an AI system
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Detrimental content
mechanism_definition: AI-generated or AI-distributed content such as deepfakes, identity misrepresentation, threats, self-harm promotion, extremist content, misinformation, sexual abuse material, or scams, which can instigate injury, damage, or loss
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Bias and differential treatment
mechanism_definition: Systematically unequal treatment of individuals or groups by an AI system, frequently with respect to protected characteristics
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Violation of privacy
mechanism_definition: Infringement of individuals' privacy interests through the collection, inference, or disclosure of personal information by an AI system
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Violation of human and civil rights
mechanism_definition: Infringement of human or civil rights attributable to the deployment or behavior of an AI system
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Violation of democratic norms
mechanism_definition: Erosion or violation of democratic processes and norms attributable to the deployment or behavior of an AI system
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Lack of validity and reliability
mechanism_definition: Failure of an AI system to produce accurate, consistent, and generalizable outputs under the conditions of expected use, including ungrounded or erroneous outputs
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Lack of safety
mechanism_definition: Operation of an AI system in ways that endanger human life, health, property, or the environment under defined conditions of use
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Lack of security and resilience
mechanism_definition: Susceptibility of an AI system to adversarial attacks, data poisoning, exfiltration, or inability to withstand and recover from adverse events
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Lack of accountability and transparency
mechanism_definition: Absence of accessible information about an AI system and of organizational structures that assign responsibility for its outcomes
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Lack of explainability and interpretability
mechanism_definition: Inability to represent the mechanisms underlying an AI system's operation or to convey the meaning of its outputs in context
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Privacy harms
mechanism_definition: Failure to safeguard the norms and practices protecting human autonomy, identity, and dignity, including unwanted disclosure or inference of personal data
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Harmful bias and unfairness
mechanism_definition: Presence of systematic computational, human-cognitive, or institutional biases in an AI system producing inequitable or discriminatory outcomes
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Physical harm (death)
mechanism_definition: An AI incident whose realized harm includes the death of a person or groups of people
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Psychological harm
mechanism_definition: Intangible harm to mental wellbeing arising from the development, use, or malfunction of an AI system
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures

label: Reputational harm
mechanism_definition: Damage to the standing or reputation of individuals, groups, or organizations attributable to an AI system
legacy_l2_reference_only: Incident harm outcomes and trustworthiness-property failures
